---
title: "slurm_sbatch_annotated"
author: "Emily Dombrowski"
date: "2026-06-01"
output: html_document
---
1 June 2026

For my australian mussel genomic data, I need to run an array that will facillitate fastq and fastp commands for trimming and cleaning. I am sure that I will need to run this type of code again, so this morning, I am spending some time making notes on some example code before starting to craft code for my own arrays. 

Here are some websites I am using as source information for this: 
* https://github.com/Jcbnunez/uvm-fqr/blob/main/Practicums/Pract4.1_Functions_Arrays_Power.md <- class website where arrays and parameter files are explained
* https://slurm.schedmd.com/sbatch.html <- slurm and sbatch specific commands for reference
* https://www.datacamp.com/blog/cpu-vs-gpu?utm_cid=22660585401&utm_aid=181540421715&utm_campaign=230119_1-ps-other~dsa-tofu~blog_2-b2c_3-nam_4-prc_5-na_6-na_7-le_8-pdsh-go_9-nb-e_10-na_11-na&utm_loc=9192404-&utm_mtd=-c&utm_kw=&utm_source=google&utm_medium=paid_search&utm_content=ps-other~nam-en~dsa~tofu~blog~data-science&gad_source=1&gad_campaignid=22660585401&gbraid=0AAAAADQ9WsGLAGgHq5SdxVbVAwRoJDYpS&gclid=Cj0KCQjw2_TQBhCnARIsAF3-Xhy8afRIERXh7f08-_bIwvxWqPpCPCzaE7t7t3gKKplZ5dcWJDDQgU8aAqivEALw_wcB <- basic information, like "what is a cpu?"

To launch an array, I think the first step is to have a consistently formatted parameter file. This file should:
* contain sample names
* have / not have numbering: the array needs to reference a specific amount of files (does the array use 0 based indexing? I beleive if numbering is inconsistent it can cause some issues)

This is an example of an array header that I will annotate. This is from Joaquin Nunez's Foundations of Quantitative reasoning class: https://github.com/Jcbnunez/uvm-fqr/blob/main/Practicums/Pract4.1_Functions_Arrays_Power.md
```{r}
#!/usr/bin/env bash  <- the "shebang"
#  
#SBATCH -J run_array  <- job title: when squeue is ran, this is what will come up
#SBATCH -c 1  <- CPU core per task 
#SBATCH -N 1 # on one node  <- specifying the number of nodes being used
#SBATCH -t 1:00:00   <- amount of time requested
#SBATCH --mem 8G   <- memory
#SBATCH -o ./slurmOutput/myarray.%A_%a.out  <- output folder. See notes
#SBATCH -p bluemoon  <- assigns job to a specific partition of the VACC
#SBATCH --array=1-10  <- launches this many copies of this script (10)
#SBATCH --account=biol6210 <- charges to a specific account

```

Notes: 
* For `#SBATCH -c`, "multi-threading" is a thing: more than one cpu can be requested per task if this is available for the packages. For fastq and fastp, it is better to use 4 cpus.
* each part of the job is spread out to one node. This makes the processing go faster.
* If your job takes longer than the allocated amount of time, it will time out.
* The output folder must exist before the job is started...this also stores error logs. The %A% situation is a placeholder that is replaced by the slurm task ID. 

Considerations for my own data set:
* Files are in two separate folders (artefact of how I specified the file path and the quantity of data: AGRF had to separate into two folders for download)
* each sequence is accross multiple lanes -- my parameter file loop will need to take this into account. Additionally, this parameter file can be used for a meta data file.
* I need the account number for our lab...unsure of how to find this

This is code from AI about how to make a parameter file: 
```
# 1. Clear out any old versions and initiate new file
> raw_list.txt

# 2. Define the source path variable
RAW_DIR="/netfiles/lockwood_lab/aus_mussels/data/raw"

# 3. Scan both subfolders for Forward reads (R1)
for f in ${RAW_DIR}/AGRF_NXGSQCAGRF25090171-1_23JW2NLT4/*_R1.fastq.gz ${RAW_DIR}/AGRF_NXGSQCAGRF25090171-2_23JW2NLT4/*_R1.fastq.gz; do
    if [ -f "$f" ]; then
        basename "$f" | cut -d'_' -f1 >> raw_list.txt
    fi
done

# 4. Deduplicate and save the clean list of 70 samples
sort -u raw_list.txt > unique_samples.txt
rm raw_list.txt

# 5. Check your work
cat -n unique_samples.txt
```

This is code that AI helped me make for running my array PLUS my annotations: 
```
#!/bin/bash <- shebang
#SBATCH --job-name=fastp_trim <- name of the job to reference
#SBATCH --output=logs/trim_%A_%a.out <- ensures named outputs in separate files
#SBATCH --error=logs/trim_%A_%a.err
#SBATCH --nodes=1
#SBATCH --ntasks=1
#SBATCH --cpus-per-task=4
#SBATCH --mem=16G
#SBATCH --time=04:00:00
#SBATCH --array=1-70 <- number of files in array

# Load fastp (equivalent to loading a library)
module load fastp

# Define raw data subfolders: loading brought 2 folders, so this is how to include both
DIR1="/netfiles/lockwood_lab/aus_mussels/data/raw/AGRF_NXGSQCAGRF25090171-1_23JW2NLT4"
DIR2="/netfiles/lockwood_lab/aus_mussels/data/raw/AGRF_NXGSQCAGRF25090171-2_23JW2NLT4"

# Save outputs into the "trimmed" folder that I am working in
OUT_DIR="."

# Extract the specific sample ID for this Slurm task
SAMPLE=$(sed -n "${SLURM_ARRAY_TASK_ID}p" unique_samples.txt)

echo "Processing task ID ${SLURM_ARRAY_TASK_ID}: Sample ${SAMPLE}"

# Determine which folder this specific sample lives in (again, remnant of two folders)
if ls ${DIR1}/${SAMPLE}_* >/dev/null 2>&1; then
    DATA_DIR=$DIR1
elif ls ${DIR2}/${SAMPLE}_* >/dev/null 2>&1; then
    DATA_DIR=$DIR2
else
    echo "ERROR: Sample ${SAMPLE} not found in either folder!"
    exit 1
fi

# Run fastp, merging all 8 lanes via bash streaming on the fly
# inputs both paired ends at the same time
# outputs html
# outputs json
# quality of trimming: 20 
fastp \
  --thread 4 \
  --in1 <(cat ${DATA_DIR}/${SAMPLE}_*_R1.fastq.gz) \
  --in2 <(cat ${DATA_DIR}/${SAMPLE}_*_R2.fastq.gz) \
  --out1 ${OUT_DIR}/${SAMPLE}_trimmed_R1.fastq.gz \
  --out2 ${OUT_DIR}/${SAMPLE}_trimmed_R2.fastq.gz \
  --html ${OUT_DIR}/${SAMPLE}_fastp.html \
  --json ${OUT_DIR}/${SAMPLE}_fastp.json \
  --qualified_quality_phred 20 \
  --length_required 30 \
  --detect_adapter_for_pe

echo "Finished processing ${SAMPLE} safely."
```
